cover.title: THE BLADE
cover.subtitle: The one who sees the evidence clearly, then turns the verdict inward.
cover.tagline: You notice what others miss, but too often, you use that precision against yourself.

profile_scores.caption: High stability and warmth, low expression. You see accurately, but apply that standard only to yourself, and stay silent.

short_summary: You do not trust quickly; you watch behavior, compare details, and notice inconsistencies earlier than most people.
Your pain often comes from turning every piece of evidence back on yourself instead of reviewing both sides fairly.
What changes love for you is learning to ask for the real reason before filling the blank with your own blame.

full_report.headline: THE BLADE
full_report.intro: You're not someone who trusts people quickly. You watch behavior, notice what doesn't line up, and see problems early. But instead of speaking that standard out loud, you often turn it inward. This report follows that pattern: why you keep reexamining yourself, why vague people pull you in, and how to review the evidence more fairly.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: Trust takes time for you.
You watch behavior. Whether what they said yesterday matches what they said today. Whether what they said matches what they did. Whether they're the same now as they were at the start.
So you notice problems pretty early. You see what others miss.
But here's where you take a different path.
Your eye for other people is precise, but you never speak that precise standard out loud.
Instead, you turn it on yourself.
If I'd just paid a little more attention, would it have turned out differently? If I'd done better, would things have changed?
You may say nothing to the other person, while going over every detail of your own behavior.
So people may see you as generous. But that isn't the whole story.
The standard didn't disappear. It just all moved over to you.
chapters.profile_note: High stability and warmth, low expression. You see accurately, but apply that standard only to yourself, and stay silent.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Repeat: THE REEXAMINATION CURVE
body: This pattern may feel familiar.
A relationship ends. Or falls apart.
In that moment, you don't blame the other person. Instead, you go back over it.
You replay the conversation. You replay the behavior. You search for where you could have done better.
It's not something you look at once and let go. You look again. And again.
It's like reopening a trial. Reviewing the same case, the same evidence, over and over.
But that inner court can keep reaching the same verdict. You gather plenty of evidence, yet your own mistakes take up most of the page.
That happens when your own behavior gets reviewed far more closely than the other person's.
chapters.highlight: The pain isn't that you saw too little. It's that you kept turning what you saw back on yourself.

chart.title: THE REEXAMINATION CURVE
chart.description: The pattern of repeatedly reviewing only your own behavior, even after the relationship has ended.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I'm someone who's too sensitive, who nitpicks too much.
items.truth: Your standards are high, that's true. But you may be using them more harshly on yourself than on anyone else.
items.truth_short: Your standards are high, but they may be aimed at you more than anyone else.

items.belief: If I'd done better, it wouldn't have ended.
items.truth: Maybe. But that isn't the only question worth asking. What did the other person do, avoid, or leave unexplained?
items.truth_short: Ask what they could have done differently too, not only what you could have changed.

items.belief: I'm not someone who blames others.
items.truth: You may not like blaming others. But when all the blame comes back to you, it still weighs just as much.
items.truth_short: Avoiding blame doesn't help if every part of it still lands on you.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You can be drawn to people who leave too much unexplained.
It may sound strange, but the pattern makes sense.
They don't say why they did what they did. Why they were late. Why they withdrew. Why they said it that way.
And you don't just sit there. You start looking for the reason yourself.
Maybe they're busy. Maybe they struggle to express themselves. Maybe something happened.
You can end up building the explanation they haven't given you.
But the explanation you build that way ends up being only your own guess. The real reason still hasn't been said by anyone.
chapters.highlight: Someone who leaves you confused keeps the reason hidden. Someone who cares about clarity helps you understand what happened.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't even harsher self examination. You already do more than enough of that.
What you need is to bring the same careful attention to the whole relationship.
When something is left unexplained, pause before filling in the reason. Ask: What did I actually hear, and what am I guessing?
A blank filled by guesswork can feel convincing, but it still isn't the same as an explanation.
Your eye is already sharp enough. Use it to check both sides before deciding what the evidence means.
chapters.action_sentence: Before judging yourself, check whether the relationship gave you enough real information.

chapters.title: Where to Go From Here
body: You know how to refine your own behavior.
You know how to go back over things. You know how to get better.
But what you'll learn next is how to review evidence fairly.
Not every blank space needs to be filled in by your own guess. Explaining is partly the other person's job too.
When both sides receive the same careful review, the relationship has more room to feel clear and less lonely.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time learning how to examine yourself. The next step is learning how to look at the whole relationship with the same fairness.

chapters.title: The Type You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: Someone Who Leaves Too Much Unsaid
items.description: You ask how their day went, and they simply smile and say, "Nothing much." The conversation ends there, but your thoughts keep going long after. Whenever a blank space opens up, you end up filling it yourself.
Last month they told it one way. Today the details feel different. You may find yourself replaying old conversations and comparing which version fits.
Their answers stay soft around the edges. Maybe. I guess. Something like that. The less defined they are, the easier it becomes to build the missing parts yourself.

items.type_title: Someone Whose Explanation Changes With the Moment
items.description: Yesterday's explanation doesn't quite match today's. You start collecting details, trying to make the pieces fit.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs may have appeared early. You may have answered them with explanations of your own.

signal_strength.label: You guess at the reason instead of asking, and move on
Even vague things, you sort out yourself and just accept them
The same question keeps coming up, but only your own explanation for it keeps getting more refined
The other person never explains directly. Only you keep analyzing

chapters.core_signal: The same question keeps returning, while your own explanation grows more detailed. The real reason still has not been clearly shared.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like to You

checklist.title: The other person tells you the reason themselves.
checklist.description: You don't have to make it up for them.

checklist.title: Your standards aren't only harsh on you.
checklist.description: The same care and scrutiny apply to both sides.

checklist.title: Explanations don't only come from one side.
checklist.description: You are not the only one responsible for making things clear.

checklist.title: The relationship feels safe even without guessing.
checklist.description: The relationship does not depend on you filling every blank alone.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're vulnerable to someone who leaves too much unexplained. Because the reason is missing, you may build one for them. The more convincing your explanation becomes, the easier it is for the real conversation to never happen.
sections.who_steadies_you: A steadier match is someone who explains what happened, keeps words and actions close, and does not leave you alone with every blank. Around someone like this, clarity does not depend on your guesses.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You may still feel drawn to someone who is hard to read. That does not mean the same pattern has to continue. What changes it is whether they meet your questions with real clarity, instead of leaving you to build the whole explanation alone.
sections.next_signs: This time, you may catch the moment sooner. Right before you start building the reason for them, ask yourself, "Did I actually hear this, or am I filling in the blank?" That pause can bring the real question back into the room.
sections.try_this: Before reviewing your own behavior, separate what you know from what you guessed. Then ask for the missing reason directly. Notice whether the other person answers clearly, stays consistent, and helps carry the conversation.
sections.final_line: The answer is not in one explanation. It is in whether clarity starts coming from both sides.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to examine yourself. What's left is learning how to examine the whole relationship fairly.
action_items: Before blaming yourself, separate what you actually heard from what you guessed.
When someone leaves something vague, ask for the reason once instead of filling it in alone.
Use the same care when reviewing their behavior that you usually use when reviewing your own.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to examine yourself. The next report covers why you always look for the cause in yourself, and how you can start sharing that examination fairly.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
